Q1: Honestly, my study routine has been pretty chaotic this term. I try to carve out about 15 hours a week, but with limited resources and juggling everything else in my life, it rarely looks the same from week to week. I'm a reader and writer by nature, so I tend to print things out or copy notes by hand when I can afford the printing costs. I usually end up studying late at night when my anxiety is somewhat manageable, though that's not always ideal.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying a bit each evening, but then something would throw me off—a bad anxiety day, money stress, whatever—and I'd fall behind. Then I'd have to cram everything into a few intense sessions before assignments were due. It's not the way I want to study, but it's the reality of my situation.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in, if I'm being honest. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements I'd missed or to see what assignments were coming up. Occasionally a classmate would message me about something, and that would remind me to check the platform. My login frequency wasn't great—maybe nine times total—because I often worked from downloaded materials instead.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way it probably should have. I'd go through phases where I'd try to check it regularly, but then my anxiety would spike or I'd get overwhelmed, and I'd avoid it for days. The peer support group actually helped remind me to engage more, but even then it felt like a conscious effort rather than a habit.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me because of my learning style. I'd download PDFs and articles, then read and annotate them on my own time. The videos were helpful too—I watched quite a bit of video content, around 79 minutes worth—but I preferred being able to read and re-read things at my own pace. Having text I could highlight and take notes from just works better for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. I know I have zero posts there, and it's not that I didn't care—I just felt too anxious to put my thoughts out there publicly. The idea of other students reading and potentially judging my contributions made my chest tight. I also delayed a lot of the optional supplementary materials because when you're struggling with time and energy, you have to prioritize what's graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to an assignment or exam. With my limited energy and high anxiety, I had to be strategic about where I invested myself. If something helped me understand a concept I knew would be tested, or if it directly contributed to an assignment I was working on, then it felt valuable. Abstract enrichment activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford.
Q8: Yes, absolutely. Required activities had this weight to them—they created pressure but also gave me direction. Optional activities felt like they existed in this nebulous space where I knew they might help, but the anxiety of falling behind on required work made me push them aside. It's frustrating because I'm sure I missed out on useful content, but when you're already barely keeping up, optional feels like a trap.
Q9: Oh, definitely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded files and handwritten notes. I'd download the readings early in the week, print what I could, and then study from those physical copies. I also used some free textbook resources I found online and borrowed books from a friend. The LMS was more like a starting point for getting materials rather than where the learning actually happened.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk with printed readings, writing out summaries and working through concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, take detailed notes, and then review those notes repeatedly over the following days—but the platform only saw that single video view. My peer interactions happened partly through the LMS but also through WhatsApp and meeting up for coffee when I could.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access a PDF or reading once, download it immediately, and then never open it on the platform again. All my subsequent engagement with that material—reading it multiple times, highlighting, making notes, testing myself on the content—happened completely offline. It was more practical for me given that I don't always have reliable internet access at home.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, usually when my anxiety was peaking or when I was stressed about money. Around midterms, I hit a wall where everything felt impossible—my attendance dropped, I was avoiding the LMS, and I felt paralyzed by how much I'd fallen behind. The peer support intervention actually came at a crucial time because having other students check in on me helped pull me back into engagement.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those factors affected me. When I was exhausted from working or stressed about bills, logging into the LMS felt like one more overwhelming thing. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them, which created this vicious cycle of anxiety and procrastination. Some weeks I'd be so fatigued that I'd stare at the screen without really processing anything, then log off feeling worse than before.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. There were times I'd open readings or videos and my mind would just be elsewhere—worrying about other things, too anxious to focus, or just mentally exhausted. I'd technically be 'engaging' with the material but absorbing almost nothing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions, and I'd have to redo them later when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My anxiety makes starting things incredibly difficult—there's this fear of doing it wrong or not being good enough that paralyzes me. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with its own stresses. The irony is that postponing made my anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have periods of low engagement, then a deadline would loom and suddenly I'd be logging in multiple times, downloading everything, frantically trying to catch up. It wasn't healthy or ideal, but the pressure of a deadline was often the only thing strong enough to override my anxiety and avoidance patterns. My assignment scores show I could produce good work—I just needed that external pressure.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback on my assignments—and I did score well on those—gave me a temporary boost and made me feel like maybe I could do this. But waiting for feedback was agonizing, and if it took too long, I'd spiral into assuming the worst. The peer support helped because getting informal feedback from classmates felt less high-stakes than waiting for official grades.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having eight online courses meant a lot of flexibility, which I needed, but also meant I had to be self-directed, which is hard when you're anxious and unmotivated. The lack of rigid scheduling meant I could work around my constraints, but it also made it easy to fall behind. I think I would have benefited from more check-ins or structured milestones.
Q19: The reading-heavy format actually worked well for my learning style, so that made engagement easier. What made it harder was the emphasis on discussion participation—my anxiety made that nearly impossible. Also, when course materials weren't clearly organized or when expectations were vague, my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid engaging altogether. Clear, straightforward instructions made a huge difference in whether I could bring myself to start something.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. They might be reading everything, watching videos, trying to understand—but then anxiety, perfectionism, or confusion prevents them from actually submitting. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that drain their energy for the final push of completing assignments. High activity doesn't always mean someone has the capacity to follow through.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me, honestly. My LMS activity was pretty low—I logged in maybe nine times—but I still managed a Merit grade because so much of my real studying happened offline. I'd download materials, study from printed notes, work through concepts on paper. The LMS captured maybe 30% of my actual engagement with the course. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on videos. That's useful for identifying students who might be completely disengaged. But it misses everything that happens offline, the quality of engagement versus just time spent, and the emotional experience of learning. It can't tell if someone is anxiously staring at a screen versus deeply focused, or if they're studying extensively from downloaded materials.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a concept and want to explore it, when I'm connecting new ideas to things I already know. For me, engagement also means pushing through my anxiety to participate, even when it's hard. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just logging hours on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected every interaction with the course—the hours I spent preparing to start an assignment, the panic before logging in, the relief when I finally submitted something. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that happened over coffee or text messages, or how much I relied on printed materials and handwritten notes. It definitely wouldn't show that despite appearing disengaged, I was actually fighting hard to stay in this course and earn my Merit.
